title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: Search target in rotated sorted array

approaches.name: Approach 1: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each element in the array sequentially to check if it matches the target. Use this when simplicity is prioritized over efficiency, as it works for any array regardless of rotation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Linear Search in Rotated Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of linear search to find target in rotated sorted array
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the linear scan approach. The intuition is straightforward: since we're prioritizing simplicity over efficiency, we can ignore the rotation property entirely and treat this as a standard search problem. The array may be rotated, but that doesn't affect a linear scan - we simply check each element from start to finish. The algorithm begins by initializing an index variable to track our position. We iterate through each element in the array sequentially. At each position, we compare the current element with the target value. If they match, we immediately return the current index. If we complete the entire loop without finding a match, we return -1 to indicate the target is not present. The time complexity is O(n) because in the worst case we examine all n elements. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra space for the index variable. This approach connects to the parent problem by providing a working solution that handles all cases, including edge cases like empty arrays, single-element arrays, and arrays with duplicates. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty arrays (should return -1), off-by-one errors in loop conditions, and not returning -1 when the target isn't found. The linear scan works regardless of rotation because we're not making any assumptions about the array's structure - we're simply examining each element in order.

Step 2: Check nums[0] = 4, not equal to 0
Step 3: i = 1, check nums[1] = 5, not equal to 0
Step 4: i = 2, check nums[2] = 6, not equal to 0
Step 5: i = 3, check nums[3] = 7, not equal to 0
Step 6: i = 4, check nums[4] = 0, equal to target
Step 7: Return index 4

Step 7: i = 5, check nums[5] = 1, not equal to 3
Step 8: i = 6, check nums[6] = 2, not equal to 3
Step 9: Loop completes, return -1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Array Traversal with Early Exit
ladders.desc: Implement linear traversal that stops when target is found
ladders.explanation: This is the core sub-routine of the linear search approach. The intuition is that we need to examine each element in order, but we can stop as soon as we find what we're looking for. This is more efficient than examining all elements when the target appears early in the array. The algorithm uses a simple for loop to iterate through array indices. At each iteration, we access the element at the current index and compare it with the target. If they match, we immediately return the current index (this is the early exit). If the loop completes without finding a match, we return -1. The time complexity is O(n) in the worst case but could be O(1) if the target is at the first position. The space complexity is O(1). This connects to the full problem by being the main search mechanism - the full problem is essentially just this sub-routine with proper input validation. Common mistakes include using the wrong comparison operator, forgetting that array indices start at 0, and not handling the case where the array is empty (should return -1 immediately). The early exit optimization is what makes this better than a full scan that always examines all elements.

examples.trace:
Step 1: i = 0, nums[0] = 2, not equal to 6
Step 2: i = 1, nums[1] = 4, not equal to 6
Step 3: i = 2, nums[2] = 6, equal to target
Step 4: Return index 2 (early exit)

Step 1: i = 0, nums[0] = 1, not equal to 7
Step 2: i = 1, nums[1] = 2, not equal to 7
Step 3: i = 2, nums[2] = 3, not equal to 7
Step 4: i = 3, nums[3] = 4, not equal to 7
Step 5: i = 4, nums[4] = 5, not equal to 7
Step 6: Loop completes, return -1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element Comparison in Sequence
ladders.desc: Compare each array element with target value sequentially
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of linear search: comparing each element in order until we find a match or exhaust the array. The intuition is that searching requires checking each candidate location, and the simplest way to do this is to check them one by one from beginning to end. We don't need to know anything about the array's structure (sorted, rotated, etc.) because we're examining every position anyway. The algorithm involves maintaining a current position index that starts at 0. We repeatedly: 1) Access the element at the current position, 2) Compare it with the target, 3) If equal, we've found our answer, 4) If not, move to the next position. This continues until we either find a match or reach the end of the array. The time complexity is O(n) because we might need to check all n elements. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the sub-routine above by being the fundamental comparison loop - the sub-routine adds the early exit optimization. Common mistakes include comparing the index instead of the element value, not incrementing the position correctly, and infinite loops when the termination condition is wrong. The key insight is that we're performing the same simple operation (comparison) repeatedly on different data elements.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Position 0, element = 3, compare with 7: not equal
Step 2: Position 1, element = 7, compare with 7: equal
Step 3: Found at position 1

Step 1: Position 0, element = 1, compare with 5: not equal
Step 2: Position 1, element = 2, compare with 5: not equal
Step 3: Position 2, element = 3, compare with 5: not equal
Step 4: Position 3, element = 4, compare with 5: not equal
Step 5: No more positions, target not found

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves understanding how to access individual elements in an array using their positions (indices). The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can retrieve any element if we know its position. In most programming languages, array indices start at 0, meaning the first element is at position 0, the second at position 1, and so on. The algorithm for accessing an element is straightforward: given an array and an index, we use the array's indexing operation (usually array[index]) to retrieve the value at that position. We need to ensure the index is within the valid range (0 to length-1) to avoid errors. The time complexity for array access is O(1) since it's a direct memory lookup. Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation itself. This connects to the core logic above because element comparison requires first accessing the element at the current position. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking the first element is at position 1), accessing out-of-bounds indices (which causes runtime errors), and confusing the index with the element value. Understanding array indexing is crucial because linear search fundamentally involves sequentially accessing array elements at different indices.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has 4 elements: positions 0,1,2,3
Step 2: Index 2 is valid (0 ≤ 2 ≤ 3)
Step 3: Access nums[2]
Step 4: Value at position 2 is 30

Step 1: Array has 3 elements: positions 0,1,2
Step 2: Index 0 is valid (0 ≤ 0 ≤ 2)
Step 3: Access nums[0]
Step 4: Value at position 0 is 5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Value Comparison
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves checking if two values are equal. The intuition is that searching requires determining whether we've found what we're looking for, which fundamentally comes down to comparing the current item with our target. The comparison operation takes two values as input and returns a boolean result: true if they are equal, false otherwise. In programming, this is typically done with the equality operator (== in many languages). The algorithm is simply: receive two values, compare them using the equality operator, return the result. The time and space complexity are both O(1) since it's a simple operation that doesn't depend on input size. This connects to array indexing above because once we access an array element, we need to compare it with our target value to determine if we've found what we're looking for. Common mistakes include using assignment (=) instead of equality comparison (==), comparing values of different types without proper conversion, and not understanding how the equality operator works for different data types (e.g., floating point comparisons). This operation is the fundamental building block of any search algorithm - without the ability to compare values, we cannot determine if we've found our target.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Take value a = 5
Step 2: Take value b = 5
Step 3: Compare 5 == 5
Step 4: Values are equal, return true

Step 1: Take value a = 7
Step 2: Take value b = 3
Step 3: Compare 7 == 3
Step 4: Values are not equal, return false

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of what an array is and how it stores data
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what an array is. An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index or key. The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values of the same type in a single variable, organized in a linear sequence. Think of it like a row of lockers: each locker has a number (index) and contains one item (element). All lockers are the same size (same data type). The key properties are: 1) Elements are stored in contiguous memory locations, 2) Each element can be accessed directly using its index, 3) The size is usually fixed when created, 4) All elements are of the same data type. The algorithm for working with arrays involves: declaring an array, initializing it with values, and accessing elements. Time complexity for accessing any element is O(1) because we can calculate its memory location directly from the index. Space complexity is O(n) to store n elements. This connects to value comparison above because once we understand arrays, we can see that searching involves comparing array elements with a target value. Common beginner mistakes include confusing arrays with other data structures, not understanding 0-based indexing, and trying to store different data types in the same array. For the search problem, the crucial understanding is that an array gives us a sequence of values we can examine one by one.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Allocate memory for 4 integers
Step 2: Store 2 at position 0
Step 3: Store 4 at position 1
Step 4: Store 6 at position 2
Step 5: Store 8 at position 3
Step 6: Array now contains [2,4,6,8]

Step 1: Array has positions 0,1,2
Step 2: Position 0 contains 10
Step 3: Position 1 contains 20
Step 4: Position 2 contains 30
Step 5: Element at position 1 is 20

approaches.name: Approach 2: Modified Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Perform binary search by identifying which half of the rotated array is sorted and adjusting the search range based on the target's position relative to the sorted half. This efficiently exploits the sorted nature despite rotation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Rotated Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Find target index in rotated sorted array using modified binary search
ladders.explanation: The problem requires searching for a target value in a rotated sorted array with O(log n) time complexity. A rotated sorted array is created by taking a sorted array and shifting some elements from the beginning to the end. The key insight is that despite the rotation, at least one half of any binary search partition will always be sorted. We can determine which half is sorted by comparing the middle element with the leftmost element. If the left half is sorted (nums[left] ≤ nums[mid]), we check if the target lies within that sorted range. If so, we search left; otherwise, we search right. If the right half is sorted (nums[mid] ≤ nums[right]), we similarly check if target is in that range. This approach maintains binary search efficiency by eliminating half the search space at each step. The algorithm initializes left=0, right=n-1, then while left ≤ right: calculate mid, return mid if nums[mid]==target. Determine which half is sorted: if nums[left] ≤ nums[mid], left half is sorted. Check if target is in [nums[left], nums[mid]]: if yes, right=mid-1; else left=mid+1. Else right half is sorted. Check if target is in [nums[mid], nums[right]]: if yes, left=mid+1; else right=mid-1. Return -1 if not found. Time: O(log n) from binary search. Space: O(1) using pointers. Connection to L1: This uses the sorted half identification logic as the core subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting equal signs in comparisons (≤ vs <), incorrect range checks for inclusive boundaries, not handling duplicates (though problem assumes distinct). Real code pattern: while left ≤ right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid]==target: return mid; if nums[left] ≤ nums[mid]: if nums[left] ≤ target < nums[mid]: right=mid-1 else: left=mid+1 else: if nums[mid] < target ≤ nums[right]: left=mid+1 else: right=mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=7 ≠ 0. nums[left]=4 ≤ nums[mid]=7 → left half sorted. target=0 not in [4,7] → left=mid+1=4. Step 2: left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=1 ≠ 0. nums[left]=0 ≤ nums[mid]=1 → left half sorted. target=0 in [0,1] → right=mid-1=4. Step 3: left=4, right=4, mid=4, nums[4]=0 == target → return 4.

Step 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=7 ≠ 3. nums[left]=4 ≤ nums[mid]=7 → left half sorted. target=3 not in [4,7] → left=mid+1=4. Step 2: left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=1 ≠ 3. nums[left]=0 ≤ nums[mid]=1 → left half sorted. target=3 not in [0,1] → left=mid+1=6. Step 3: left=6, right=6, mid=6, nums[6]=2 ≠ 3. nums[left]=2 ≤ nums[mid]=2 → left half sorted. target=3 not in [2,2] → left=mid+1=7. Step 4: left=7 > right=6 → exit loop, return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Identify Sorted Half in Rotated Array
ladders.desc: Determine which half of a rotated sorted array partition is sorted
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the modified binary search approach. Given a rotated sorted array and indices left, mid, right representing a partition, we need to identify whether the left half [left, mid] or right half [mid, right] is sorted. The intuition: in a rotated sorted array, at least one half of any partition will be completely sorted because the rotation creates a single pivot point. We compare nums[left] and nums[mid]: if nums[left] ≤ nums[mid], then the left half is sorted (including equal for single elements). Otherwise, the right half must be sorted (since at least one half is sorted). This works because in a sorted array segment, the first element is ≤ the last element. After rotation, the pivot breaks this property for one half. Time: O(1) constant comparison. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This logic is used in each binary search iteration to decide where to search next. Common mistakes: using strict inequality (<) instead of ≤, which fails for single-element halves; not considering that both halves could be sorted in unrotated arrays (but ≤ handles this). Real code pattern: if nums[left] ≤ nums[mid]: left_half_sorted = True else: left_half_sorted = False.

examples.trace: nums[left]=4, nums[mid]=7. 4 ≤ 7 is true → left half [0,3] is sorted (values [4,5,6,7] are increasing).
nums[left]=0, nums[mid]=1. 0 ≤ 1 is true → left half [4,5] is sorted (values [0,1] are increasing).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search in Sorted Half
ladders.desc: Search for target in sorted portion of array using binary search conditions
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves deciding which half of the array to search based on whether the target lies within the sorted half. Once we identify which half is sorted (from L1), we check if the target is within that sorted half's range. If it is, we narrow the search to that half; otherwise, we search the other half. The intuition: if the target is within the bounds of the sorted half, it must be there (if present) because the sorted half is contiguous and monotonic. If not, it must be in the other half, which may contain the rotation pivot. For left half sorted: if nums[left] ≤ target < nums[mid], search left (right=mid-1); else search right (left=mid+1). For right half sorted: if nums[mid] < target ≤ nums[right], search right (left=mid+1); else search left (right=mid-1). Note the strict inequalities for mid to avoid redundancy since mid is already checked. Time: O(1) per decision. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: Uses sorted half identification to apply this logic. Common mistakes: using ≤ instead of < for mid comparisons causing infinite loops, incorrect inclusive/exclusive bounds. Real code pattern: if left_half_sorted: if nums[left] ≤ target < nums[mid]: right=mid-1 else: left=mid+1 else: if nums[mid] < target ≤ nums[right]: left=mid+1 else: right=mid-1.

examples.input: nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], left=0, mid=3, right=6, target=0, left_half_sorted=True
examples.trace: Left half [0,3] sorted with values [4,5,6,7]. Check if target=0 in [4,7]: 4 ≤ 0 < 7 is false → target not in left half, so search right half (set left=mid+1=4).

examples.input: nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], left=4, mid=5, right=6, target=0, left_half_sorted=True
examples.trace: Left half [4,5] sorted with values [0,1]. Check if target=0 in [0,1]: 0 ≤ 0 < 1 is true → target in left half, so search left (set right=mid-1=4).

testCases.input: nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], left=0, mid=3, right=6, target=0, left_half_sorted=True
nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], left=4, mid=5, right=6, target=0, left_half_sorted=True
nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], left=0, mid=3, right=6, target=5, left_half_sorted=True
nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], left=2, mid=4, right=6, target=1, left_half_sorted=False
nums = [1,2,3,4,5], left=0, mid=2, right=4, target=3, left_half_sorted=True
nums = [5,1,2,3,4], left=0, mid=2, right=4, target=4, left_half_sorted=False
nums = [3,4,5,1,2], left=0, mid=2, right=4, target=1, left_half_sorted=True
nums = [2,3,4,5,1], left=0, mid=2, right=4, target=1, left_half_sorted=True
nums = [1], left=0, mid=0, right=0, target=1, left_half_sorted=True
nums = [2,1], left=0, mid=0, right=1, target=1, left_half_sorted=True

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Midpoint in Array Range
ladders.desc: Calculate middle index for binary search partition
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the midpoint index between two indices left and right, crucial for dividing the search space in binary search. The standard method is mid = (left + right) // 2 using integer division to get the floor. The intuition: binary search works by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half; the midpoint serves as the pivot for comparison and partition. Using floor division ensures we get a valid integer index. For even-sized ranges, this picks the left-middle element. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This midpoint is used to identify sorted half and apply search logic. Common mistakes: using floating-point division or not using integer division, causing type errors; overflow in languages with fixed integers (use left + (right-left)//2 to avoid). Real code pattern: mid = (left + right) // 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Array Element with Target
ladders.desc: Check if array element equals target value
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation compares an element in an array at a given index with a target value to determine if they are equal. It's the basic check in search algorithms. The intuition: searching involves examining elements one by one (or via partitions) to find a match; this comparison is the atomic action. Use == operator for equality check. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: After finding midpoint, we compare nums[mid] with target to see if search is complete. Common mistakes: using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==), not handling different data types properly. Real code pattern: if nums[mid] == target: return mid.

examples.trace: nums[3] = 7, target = 7, 7 == 7 is true.
nums[3] = 7, target = 0, 7 == 0 is false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Access element in array using index
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: accessing an element in an array by its index. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each identifiable by a zero-based index. The intuition: arrays allow random access; given an index i, we can directly retrieve the element at position i in constant time. In most programming languages, use syntax like nums[i] to access. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This operation enables comparing elements with targets. Common mistakes: using out-of-bounds indices (e.g., negative or ≥ array length), confusing zero-based with one-based indexing. Real code pattern: element = nums[index].

examples.trace: Array nums has elements: index 0 → 4, 1 → 5, 2 → 6, 3 → 7, 4 → 0, 5 → 1, 6 → 2. Access nums[0] returns 4.
Access nums[4] returns 0.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Find Pivot Then Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: First find the pivot index where the array is rotated using binary search, then perform binary search on the appropriate sorted half. This separates the rotation detection and search steps for clarity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Rotated Sorted Array Using Pivot-First Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution: find pivot index using binary search, then search target in appropriate sorted half.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the rotated sorted array search problem in O(log n) time by separating it into two distinct binary search phases. First, we identify the pivot index where the rotation occurs—this is the index of the minimum element in the array. The pivot divides the array into two sorted subarrays. Once we have the pivot, we determine which sorted half could contain our target based on comparison with the first element. Then we perform a standard binary search on that half. The intuition is that even though the array is rotated, we can still leverage binary search by first understanding the rotation structure. The pivot finding works because in a rotated sorted array, the left half (from start to pivot-1) and right half (from pivot to end) are individually sorted. By comparing mid with the first element, we can determine which side is properly sorted and adjust our search accordingly. Time complexity is O(log n) for both phases combined, space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the pivot-finding subroutine as the first step. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in pivot calculation, incorrect handling of edge cases like single-element arrays, and forgetting to check if the array is not rotated at all. Real code pattern: def search(nums, target): pivot = find_pivot(nums); if target >= nums[0]: return binary_search(nums, 0, pivot-1, target); else: return binary_search(nums, pivot, len(nums)-1, target).

examples.trace: Step 1: Find pivot. left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[mid]=7 >= nums[0]=4, so left=mid+1=4. left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[mid]=1 < nums[0]=4, so right=mid-1=4. left=4, right=4, pivot=4. Step 2: Determine which half to search. target=0 < nums[0]=4, so search right half from pivot=4 to end=6. Step 3: Binary search on [0,1,2]. left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[mid]=1 > target=0, so right=mid-1=4. left=4, right=4, nums[mid]=0 == target, return 4.

Step 1: Find pivot. left=0, right=1, mid=0, nums[mid]=1 >= nums[0]=1, so left=mid+1=1. left=1, right=1, pivot=1. Step 2: Determine which half to search. target=0 < nums[0]=1, so search right half from pivot=1 to end=1. Step 3: Binary search on [3]. left=1, right=1, nums[mid]=3 != target, return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Pivot Index in Rotated Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Binary search to locate the index of the minimum element (pivot) in a rotated sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine finds the pivot index, which is the position of the smallest element in a rotated sorted array. The intuition is that in such an array, all elements from the start to the pivot-1 are sorted and greater than or equal to the first element, while elements from pivot to end are sorted and less than the first element. By using binary search, we compare the middle element with the first element: if nums[mid] >= nums[0], the left half is sorted, and the pivot must be to the right; otherwise, the pivot is to the left or at mid. This reduces the search space by half each time. Time complexity is O(log n), space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the pivot needed to decide which half to search for the target. Common mistakes include not handling arrays that are not rotated (pivot at 0), infinite loops due to incorrect boundary updates, and off-by-one errors in the final pivot assignment. Real code pattern: def find_pivot(nums): left=0, right=len(nums)-1; while left<right: mid=left+(right-left)//2; if nums[mid]>=nums[0]: left=mid+1; else: right=mid; return left.

examples.trace: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[mid]=7 >= nums[0]=4, so left=mid+1=4. left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[mid]=1 < nums[0]=4, so right=mid=5. left=4, right=5, mid=4, nums[mid]=0 < nums[0]=4, so right=mid=4. left=4, right=4, loop ends, return left=4.

left=0, right=3, mid=1, nums[mid]=2 >= nums[0]=1, so left=mid+1=2. left=2, right=3, mid=2, nums[mid]=3 >= nums[0]=1, so left=mid+1=3. left=3, right=3, loop ends, return left=3? Wait, check: for non-rotated array, pivot should be 0. Actually, algorithm returns left=3, but we need to handle: if nums[left] >= nums[0], pivot=0 else pivot=left. Here nums[3]=4 >= nums[0]=1, so pivot=0. Correction in trace: after loop, if nums[left] >= nums[0]: return 0 else return left. So output 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Standard binary search algorithm to find a target in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental technique of binary search, which efficiently locates a target in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition is that because the array is sorted, we can compare the target with the middle element: if they match, we found it; if the target is smaller, it must be in the left half; if larger, in the right half. This reduces the problem size by half each step, leading to O(log n) time complexity and O(1) space. It connects to L1 and L0 as the second phase after pivot finding, applied to the appropriate sorted half. Common mistakes include incorrect calculation of mid (leading to overflow), off-by-one errors in updating left/right pointers, and not handling cases where the target is not present. Real code pattern: def binary_search(nums, left, right, target): while left<=right: mid=left+(right-left)//2; if nums[mid]==target: return mid; elif nums[mid]<target: left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: left=0, right=2, mid=1, nums[mid]=1 == target, return 1.
left=0, right=2, mid=1, nums[mid]=1 < target, so left=mid+1=2. left=2, right=2, mid=2, nums[mid]=2 < target, so left=mid+1=3. left=3, right=2, loop ends, return -1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare and Halve Search Space
ladders.desc: Using comparison to decide which half of an array to search next.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves making a decision based on comparing a target or key with a middle element to eliminate half of the current search space. The intuition is that in ordered data, such comparisons provide enough information to confidently discard one half, dramatically reducing the number of elements to consider. This is the core idea behind divide-and-conquer algorithms like binary search. Time complexity for a single step is O(1), but when applied iteratively, it leads to logarithmic overall time. It connects to L2 as the repeated action within binary search. Common mistakes include comparing with the wrong element or misinterpreting the sort order. Real code pattern: if key < arr[mid]: search in left half; elif key > arr[mid]: search in right half; else: found.

examples.input: arr = [10,20,30,40,50], key = 30, current range indices 0 to 4
examples.output: search in whole range, compare with mid
examples.trace: Mid index = (0+4)//2 = 2, arr[2] = 30. Compare key=30 with arr[2]=30: equal, so found at index 2. No halving needed here since match, but if not equal, e.g., key=25: key=25 < arr[2]=30, so eliminate right half (indices 3-4), new range 0-1.

examples.input: arr = [5,15,25], key = 20, current range indices 0 to 2
examples.trace: Mid index = (0+2)//2 = 1, arr[1] = 15. Compare key=20 with arr[1]=15: key > arr[mid], so eliminate left half (indices 0-0), new range 2-2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Calculate Mid Index Without Overflow
ladders.desc: Safely compute the middle index of a range using integer arithmetic.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation computes the midpoint between two indices, left and right, without risking integer overflow. The intuition is that instead of using (left + right) // 2, which can overflow if left and right are large integers, we use left + (right - left) // 2. This ensures the sum stays within bounds by first calculating the difference. It's a critical step in binary search and other divide-and-conquer algorithms. Time and space are O(1). It connects to L3 as part of the process to find the middle element for comparison. Common mistakes include using floating-point division or incorrect integer division. Real code pattern: mid = left + (right - left) // 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices, starting from 0.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how to access individual elements in an array using their indices. In most programming languages, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The intuition is that an array is a contiguous block of memory, and indices act as offsets from the start to quickly retrieve values. This foundational skill is essential for any algorithm that manipulates arrays, including binary search. Time complexity for access is O(1), space is O(1). It connects to L4 as we need to access the element at the mid index. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing by accident or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: element = arr[index].

examples.trace: Array arr has elements: index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30. Access arr[1] returns the value at index 1, which is 20.
Array arr: index 0 = 5, index 1 = 15, index 2 = 25, index 3 = 35. Access arr[3] returns 35.
